summary: Gruber publishes his annual Macworld Expo 2009 predictions, covering expected hardware updates, software releases, and iPhone rumors. He correctly predicts the 17-inch MacBook Pro and iLife/iWork '09, but misses on several others including Mac Mini, Cinema Display, Snow Leopard demo, and Apple TV updates. The post is notable for his extended analysis of Apple TV's long-term strategy and his argument that downloadable movies will eventually replace physical media. He also lays out a detailed wish list for iPhone OS 3.0, including a better home screen, background notifications, and copy-and-paste.
key_points: Gruber correctly predicted the 17-inch MacBook Pro with sealed battery and iLife/iWork '09, but was wrong on most other hardware predictions including Mac Mini, Cinema Display, iMac, and Apple TV updates.
He argues Apple has a long-term iPod-like growth strategy for Apple TV, noting the market for living room digital media has no dominant player yet.
He makes a prescient case that DVD or Blu-ray will be the last mainstream physical medium for home movies, with downloads being the inevitable future.
His iPhone OS 3.0 wish list identifies real pain points — home screen management for dozens of apps, background notifications, and copy-and-paste — all of which Apple eventually addressed.
He dismisses iPhone Nano rumors as nonsensical given the iPhone 3G was only six months old, questioning why Apple would use 'nano' branding for something only slightly smaller.
money_quotes: As required by the FCC, all Mac-related web sites must publish pre-Macworld Expo predictions regarding what Apple may announce at the show.
I don't think there's any great enthusiasm for the Mac Mini at Apple, but it's a strong seller.
20-inch displays are the new 17-inch displays: too small.
If DVD isn't the last mainstream physical medium for home movie distribution, Blu-ray will be. The future, obviously and inevitably, is in downloads.

The iPhone was an instant hit, but the iPod wasn't. Apple grew the iPod from a Mac-only peripheral into a cultural sensation slowly but steadily over three or four years. I think they have a similar long-term plan for Apple TV.
Managing dozens of apps on the iPhone today is simply a pain in the ass.
I've made this prediction several times in the past and been wrong, but eventually I'll be right: it's time for the last vestiges of the original Mac OS X 10.0 'Aqua' theme to go.
key_insight: Gruber's predictions reveal how Apple's product strategy operates on long timelines — the company plants seeds like Apple TV and sealed batteries that only make sense years later, even when critics dismiss them as mistakes.
